author: @stevecummins324
text:
have a try dipping anodised aluminimum of titantium sheet into a tap water filled metal cup. wave motion, or dripping water would vary capacitance.

the anodised layer has a very high dielectric constant, is very thin, contibuting to high capacitance per unit area.  the anodized layers have very high resistance, and thus the resistance of the water can in practice be ignored. plate area is the area in contact with the water.

anodizing to increased voltage will increase thickness of the layer... anodize to say 5V until current drops off and in effect 5V is the breakdown voltage.  but any over voltage will just restart anodizing.

author: @markflint9089
text: You could perhaps create an "electronic" variable capacitance by switching several capacitor plates in and out of series arrangements, and so increasing/decreasing the plate area. There might be a fancy Integrated Circuit these days for that.

replies.author: @johnc3403
replies.text: A simple diode does the same thing when a reverse DC voltage is applied to it. The capacitance is proportional to the neg. voltage applied. There is a type of diode called a varicap diode that is actually designed for this purpose and it is used in the tuning stages of more modern radio receivers. Not so fancy and modern as you might think though,  Varicap diodes were first patented in 1961.

replies.author: @markflint9089
replies.text: @johnc3403 Thanks for that. My electronics is rudimentary so I tend to switch most things through transistors and a microcontroller! One day I would like to experiment with what you're talking about.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
The first generator you show around the turntable motor coil is not a vailable generator concept. Look at my and Jason Joplin's comment in the "Playing With Coils And Magnets" video. Only when washers with interleaved tabs are used as present in the turntable motor it will do a reasonable job. Otherwise it cannot.
The second generator in the video is more interesting. I think several things are going on. Your plates are a good antenna for electrostatic fields to start with. 50/60 Hz fields are always present and will generate a fair voltage all by itself.Then indeed if you have a charge on the capacitor and if you lower the value of the capacitor the voltage goes up as charge will not change. ( V = Q/C ). Add to that you are performing work as the two plates have opposite charge, attract each other and you are pulling them apart. I think this energy is added to the charge so will contribute to the voltage. Of course when the voltage gets higher than the voltage on your buffer capacitor with the diode thresholds added then energy will be "trapped" as you indicate. So moving the plates back together does not reverse the process.
It would be interesting if you remove all load on you buffer capacitor ( remove the voltage meter ) and wait for a few minutes. Then reattach the multimeter, you should get a fair voltage but dropping fast. Instead of the multimeter attach a led to the buffer capacitor. I think it will flash. It's not free energy. The electrostatic fields which caused it are due to your mains wires and add to your electricity bill. ( be it very little )

author: @esccontroldeletecommand2229
text: In the days of Benjamin Franklin electricity was known as Electric fluidics. Since water is magnetic it can help you with understanding why it's flowing towards the lowest pressure, being accelerated towards the metal.

replies.author: @elams1894
replies.text: I don't believe anything is flowing in an electrical system. The model of fluid dynamics falls apart as soon as capacitors are involved. In addition, the concept of current falls apart when capacitors are involved. It is the unique reaction of different elements as well as some compound materials, to a changing magnetic field that is most crucial in this system. Induction is the key mechanism it seems.

replies.author: @esccontroldeletecommand2229
replies.text: @elams1894 except for the fact that a capacitor is also an inductor and inductor is also a capacitor.

replies.author: @elams1894
replies.text: @esccontroldeletecommand2229 I agree that induction is certainly occurring in both components. In an inductor, the ferrite is induced by the magnetic field of the copper coil surrounding it, thereby moving the bloc wall of the magnetic field. In a capacitor, any movement of magnetic field is induced from one plate to the other. I'm not sure how that relates to fluid dynamics however. Cheers.

Your idea is very similar to something I recently stumbled across. I read about a generator that uses a graphene membrane to convert Brownian motion into electricity. The graphene membrane acts like one plate of a variable capacitor. The membrane is deformed by molecules agitated by Brownian motion. The cells were microscopic and the voltage was minuscule but enough cells running in parallel could be useful. Another magical application of graphene if we ever find a way to manufacture things with it. 
Graphene and fusion reactors seem to have a lot in common in that respect.

author: @TerryGilsenan
text:
What is the forward voltage drop of the diodes you are using? The electron source would need to be higher voltage than the forward voltage drop in order for there to be and actual flow of electrons through he PN junction... so the actual peak voltages at the plates themselves are around .7 volts + whatever voltage you are registering on the voltage meter... meaning that the electron sources are even more efficient that suspected, and changing to a very low voltage signal diode rather than a power diode may actually harvest more energy because the forward voltage drop is less.

You could use different diodes, because the actual amount of electrons flowing through the junction is very low. (low amperage) so a scalpel might be more appropriate than a hammer for a jule harvesting electron pump.

Its also quite possible that I haven't the slightest idea what I am talking about :)

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Try this variable capacitor/electron pump. 
1) Paint graphene to two sides of a rubber sheet.
2) Secure one end and attach a weight to the other.  When you stretch it, the plates both grow in area, and the distance between them decreases. Together both effects increases the capacitance.  
3) Use a pvc pipe and wool to  electrostatic induction charge one plate, while the other plate is connected to ground through a load. 
4) Pull and release the weight and rubber sheet/band causes the weight to oscillate as if it were attached to a spring, but  electrons will be pushed and pulled through your load dampening the vibrations, as energy is transfered to the load.

if the load is a full bridge rectifier and capacitor, then the capacitor will store a charge, potentially upto a few hundred to a thousand volts or so given the high voltage in electrostatic charge. the ammount of energy per oscillation depends on the the voltage from electrostatic induction amount of (absolute vs relative) change in capacitance. 

As to capacitors as electron traps.  a capacitor doesnt really "trap" electrons so much as they store energy through storing high concentration of electron on one plate with a low concentration on the other.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text: I think you mean you are unsure that he is not actually seeing triboelectric electrostatic effects, versus electrostatic induction as seen in an electrophorus.

replies.author: @1967spark
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 It may be either, that's why I wondered what would happen within a Faraday cage.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@1967spark I believe what he is seeing is a little bit of both, with the triboelectric effect providing the electrostatic charges needed for electrostatic induction.   

as to a faraday cage, it is not going to do anything to stop triboelectric  effects, nor the electrostatic induction seen in an electrophorus.
